title: A page that receives
testCase: Is there a <form> with an action and a method?
blocks.0.text: Everything you have built so far has gone one way: you show, the person looks. The page gives. It does not take.
blocks.1.text: Now it turns around. You want the person to write you something and have it reach you.
blocks.2.text: You made a field and a button. It looks like a form. Run it and try: type something in the field, press Send.
blocks.3.text: Nothing. The field took your text. The button pressed. And the data went nowhere — because there is nowhere for it to go.
blocks.4.text: A few fields and a button are not a form. They are a few fields and a button.
blocks.6.text: What is missing is the tag that BINDS them and says what should happen to them. <form> answers two questions the fields cannot answer on their own:
blocks.7.items.0: WHERE to send them — the action attribute. The address that will receive them.
blocks.7.items.1: HOW to send them — the method attribute. The way they travel.
blocks.9.text: Now the button means something. Press it and the browser gathers everything in the form, wraps it up and sends it to the address in action.
blocks.11.text: Did you notice name="email" on the field? It is mandatory and people constantly forget it. Without it, the field is not sent at all.
blocks.12.text: Think about why. The data arrives at the server as pairs: a name and a value. "email: ivan@abv.bg". If the field has no name, the value arrives with no label — like posting a letter without writing who it is for. The server throws it away.
blocks.13.text: A field with no name is a field that does not exist.
blocks.16.text: Two ways to send, and the difference is not a technical whim:
blocks.17.items.0: GET — the data travels IN THE ADDRESS. You see it up in the bar: ?email=ivan@abv.bg. So does anyone looking at your screen. It goes into the history.
blocks.17.items.1: POST — the data travels HIDDEN, in the body of the request. The address stays clean.
blocks.18.text: The rule is simple: if the form READS something (a search, a filter) — GET. The address becomes shareable, you can bookmark it. If the form CHANGES something (a signup, an order, a message) — POST.
blocks.19.text: And never GET for a password. It will sit in the address bar, go into the browser history and into the server logs. I have seen it on real sites.
blocks.21.text: Honestly: the address /send does not exist. There is nobody to receive your data. The form will send it and get nothing back.
blocks.22.text: That is normal and it is not your job. HTML writes the form. What happens to the data afterwards — that is the server, another language, another course. You are building the hand that offers.
blocks.23.text: Your turn. Turn the field and the button into a real form that knows where and how to send. And give the field a name.
